region_of_origin: China (Beijing); widely served in Singapore Chinese restaurants

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Peking roast duck (duck only). FOODON_03302051 (duck food product) is used as the closest broader class anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ (duck meat, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No specific Peking-style roasted duck code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024; match_type set to closest_parent as the code represents the broader cooked duck meat category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Peking roast duck (duck only) as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 150 g, duck meat only, no pancake or sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Peking duck meat only is approximately 150 g, representing sliced roasted duck flesh with crispy skin but without the accompanying pancakes, hoisin sauce, or spring onion garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Peking Duck (Duck Only) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11040007 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All seven core nutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and potassium are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05143 (duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted) as HPB does not separately report these values for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for high-protein roasted poultry/duck
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Peking duck (duck only) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the analogous category of high-protein, low-carbohydrate roasted poultry dishes. The carbohydrate content of 5.24 g/100g is primarily from marinade sugars (maltose/hoisin); protein and fat dominate the macronutrient profile and substantially blunt any glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (30 × 5.24 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 2.4, rounded to 2.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 363 mg/100g and high saturated fat (~6.5 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Peking duck to a small portion (≤100 g) and request preparation without additional soy or hoisin marinade to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal; the saturated fat content also contributes to arterial stiffness and should be factored into daily saturated fat targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and high protein (22.06 g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Peking duck (duck only) is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (100–150 g) as the low carbohydrate and high protein content support glycaemic control; however, accompanying hoisin sauce should be avoided as it adds significant sugar and carbohydrates, and total fat intake should be monitored given the 22.2 g/100g fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~6.5 g/100g, cholesterol ~84 mg/100g, and sodium 363 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume Peking duck (duck only) occasionally and in small portions (≤100 g), removing the skin where possible to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol load, and avoiding high-sodium condiments to limit cumulative cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~6.5 g/100g exceeds 5 g/100g RA threshold; trace trans fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6) from duck are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Peking duck (duck only) due to its high saturated fat content, trace trans fat generated during high-heat roasting, and arachidonic acid content in duck meat, all of which promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity and may worsen joint inflammation; removing the crispy skin significantly reduces AGE and saturated fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peking Duck (Duck Only) is the roasted duck meat component of the iconic Chinese dish Beijing Roast Duck (北京烤鸭), served without the traditional accompaniments of thin pancakes, hoisin sauce, spring onions, or cucumber. The duck is prepared by coating the whole bird in a maltose-based glaze with soy sauce, five-spice powder, ginger, and garlic, then air-drying and roasting at high heat in a wood-fired or conventional oven to achieve the characteristic lacquered, mahogany-coloured crispy skin and tender, flavourful meat. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and slightly sweet from the glaze, with a pronounced umami depth. The texture contrasts between the crackling crispy skin and the moist, succulent duck flesh beneath. Nutritionally, per 100 g this dish provides 309 kcal, 22.06 g protein, 22.2 g fat (of which approximately 6.5 g is saturated), and only 5.24 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate food. Sodium is 363 mg/100g, reflecting the soy and marinade contribution. The low carbohydrate content means the glycaemic impact is minimal (estimated GI ~30, low category), making it broadly compatible with low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns when consumed without high-sugar sauces. Clinically, the saturated fat and sodium content are the primary concerns for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. In Singapore, Peking duck is widely served at Chinese restaurants and is a popular festive and celebratory dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Peking duck?
Is Peking duck high in sodium?
Is Peking duck suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a review_status of reviewed and input confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and potassium are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05143 (duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for the fatty acid profile of roasted duck. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for high-protein, low-carbohydrate roasted poultry.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.5 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (9.7 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (2.9 g/100g), trans fat (0.1 g/100g), cholesterol (84 mg/100g), and potassium (280 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05143 (duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted) as the HPB Foodo record does not separately report these values. These are approximate and may vary with preparation method and duck breed. 2. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB Peking duck (duck only) preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the level of specificity required; USDA NDB 05143 provides some micronutrient data but the match type is closest_parent rather than exact, and values are not carried forward to avoid overstating precision. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) based on the high-protein, low-carbohydrate roasted poultry category from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Peking duck (duck only) exists in the approved GI source as of 2026. 4. Halal status is marked false as traditional Peking duck preparation uses non-halal-certified duck and soy-based marinades; halal-certified versions exist in Singapore but are not the default assumption for this generic record. 5. This record covers duck meat only (with skin as served); nutritional values will differ significantly if skin is removed or if the dish is served with pancakes, hoisin sauce, and accompaniments. 6. ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
